1: Belshatzar malka ʿabad lechem rab (bēlšaṣṣar malkāʾ ʿăḇaḏ leḥem raḇ): Belshazzar the king made a great feast. Historical note: Belshazzar is now confirmed as a genuine Babylonian figure through the Nabonidus Chronicle and the Verse Account of Nabonidus — he served as co-regent with his father Nabonidus. His title is son of Nebuchadnezzar (Daniel 5:2, 11) in the Semitic sense of dynasty-descendant or successor, not literal son. The feast of a thousand: alef rabrevaneh (a thousand of his nobles) — the hyperbolic number of royal grandeur. The historical night is likely October 539 BCE when Cyrus's troops diverted the Euphrates and entered Babylon.
2: taam (ṭĕʿēm): taste/order/edict. The Aramaic taam (taste/judgment/decree) suggests a royal command issued under the influence of wine. The vessels of the Jerusalem temple (mano di-hekhla di-beyt-elaha di-birushalem) are specifically identified: mano (vessels/utensils), hekhla (temple). The desecration of the sacred vessels connects to Nebuchadnezzar's original seizure (Daniel 1:2; cf. Ezra 1:7-11) — the vessels taken as war-trophies are now pressed into profane banquet use, escalating the sacrilege.
3: The active desecration: v'eshtu vahon malka virabrevaneh (and they drank from them, the king and his nobles). The plural includes the concubines and wives (shegalateh unilkhateh). The drinking scene is a deliberate contrast with the earlier narrative: Nebuchadnezzar at least worshipped a god (the gold image); Belshazzar reduces worship to a feast-prop. The vessels become vessels of judgment: what was used to defile becomes the occasion for divine response.
4: shabbahu le-elahei dahava vekhaspah (šabbaḥû lēʾlāhê dahabāʾ wĕḵasp̄āʾ): they praised the gods of gold and silver. The list of six materials (gold, silver, bronze, iron, wood, stone) mirrors the statue of Dan 2 — deliberately invoking Nebuchadnezzar's dream and its interpretation. The six materials correspond to the six kingdoms of the Daniel 2 vision. The feast that praises these material gods while desecrating YHWH's temple-vessels is the concentrated expression of what Daniel 2 condemned: human kingdoms that mistake their own material glory for ultimate reality.
5: etsba din alaha (ʾeṣḇāʾ dî yad ʾĕnāšāʾ): fingers of a man's hand. The vision emerges from the wall: ketefat gira di-kedasha di-hechal malka (opposite the lampstand, on the plaster of the wall of the king's palace). The lampstand (nehora, light) illuminates the scene — the writing appears where it can be seen by all. The Aramaic pars khetav (wrote writing) emphasizes both the action and the product: the divine hand writes and the writing stands.
6: be-edin malka zivohi shennoi (bēʾḏayin malkāʾ ziwnôhî šennaw): then the king's countenance changed. The verb shennoi (was changed) is the same root used in 3:19 for Nebuchadnezzar's changed countenance and in 4:16 for the changed heart — Daniel's Aramaic consistently uses this root for radical transformation under divine pressure. Reʾyaneh yevahaloneh (his thoughts alarmed him): the verb behal (to terrify/disturb) appears throughout the chapter (Daniel 5:9, 10) — the same root as Daniel 4's king-in-terror. The physical symptoms — loosened loins, knocking knees — are the diplomatic language for total collapse of composure.
7: Belshazzar cries out for the same ashpaya vegadzraya khaldaya (astrologers, Chaldeans, diviners) who have failed in every previous crisis (Daniel 2, 4). The reward offered is: arganev-an talbash v'hemnikhta di-dahava al-tzawareh (clothed in purple and a chain of gold around his neck) and telita b'malkhuta ishalat (third ruler in the kingdom) — consistent with Belshazzar's position as co-regent: he can only offer third place, not second. This is historically precise and archaeologically confirmed.
8: The failure of the wise men: la yakhallin khetava lemeqra (could not read the writing) or give interpretation. The Aramaic structure parallels Daniel 2 and Daniel 4 — the court wisdom system fails identically each time. The literary pattern says: there is a category of knowledge that human wisdom systems cannot access. Peshara lehodat (to make known the interpretation) — the verb ydaʿ (to know/make known) is the epistemological key: Babylonian wisdom cannot produce the knowledge YHWH alone gives.
9: be-edin malka Belshatzar saggi metvahel (ʾeḏayin malkāʾ bēlšaṣṣar saggî mĕṯbahal): then King Belshazzar was greatly troubled. The superlative saggi (greatly) intensifies the already-intense verb. Zivohi shannyin ʿalohay (his countenance was changing upon him) — again the root shannyi. V'rabrevaneh metvahalin (and his nobles were troubled) — the fear spreads from the king to the court. The banquet that began with triumph ends in terror: the vessels of YHWH have become the vessels of judgment.
10: malketa liqbel milei malka v-rabrevaneh leveyt mishtaya ʿalit (malkĕṯāʾ liqbēl millê malkāʾ wĕrabrĕwānôhî lĕḇêṯ mišĕtāyāʾ ʿāllat): the queen, because of the words of the king and his nobles, came into the banqueting hall. The queen who enters is likely the queen-mother — Nitocris or a senior queen with knowledge of Nebuchadnezzar's court (Daniel 5:11: “in the days of your father”). Her composure — malketa lealam kheyey (O king, live forever) — contrasts with the general alarm. She has information the current court lacks.
11: itai gever b'malkhutakh di-ruach elahim qadishim beh (ʾîṯay gĕḇar bĕmalkûṯāk dî-rûaḥ ʾĕlāhîn qaddîšîn bēhî): there is a man in your kingdom in whom is the spirit of the holy gods. The queen-mother's testimony establishes Daniel's credentials by reference to Nebuchadnezzar's own evaluation. Three terms are used for Nebuchadnezzar's recognition of Daniel: nahor (light/illumination), sekhlet (insight), and khokhmat elahim (wisdom of the gods) — the same terms appear in the original court examination (Daniel 1:20). The spirit that was present in Nebuchadnezzar's day is still present in Daniel: divine gifts are permanent, not generational.
12: ruach yatira v'mandaʿ v'sekhlet lefeysher khelmin (rûaḥ yattîrāh ûmandaʿ ûśokletānû lĕp̄ašar ḥelmîn): an extraordinary spirit and knowledge and understanding to interpret dreams. The list of Daniel's abilities: interpreting dreams, solving riddles (mefesha udah), and loosening knots (meshare qatrein). The Aramaic qatrein (knots/difficulties) is a vivid metaphor for complex problems — the same image appears in modern Arabic. Daniel's name is given by the queen: Belteshatzar (his court name) — she uses it to identify him to a king who may not have known him personally.
13: Daniel is brought before the king. The king's address begins with a biographical note: ant-hu Daniel di-min-benei galuta di-yehud (are you Daniel who is from the exiles of Judah?). The identification of Daniel as an exile (galuta) is politically precise: he has no natural power-base, no dynastic claim, no court loyalty by birth. His status as exile makes his authority all the more clearly supernatural. The king knows Daniel's origin — this is not ignorance but the beginning of an interrogation designed to assess reliability.
14: The king's statement of what he has heard about Daniel: v'shemet ʿalakh di ruach elahim bakh (and I have heard of you that the spirit of God is in you). The king uses elahim (God/gods) without qualification here — a more personal phrasing than the queen-mother's elahim qadishim (holy gods). The three-part description of Daniel's gifts: nahor v'sekhlet v'khokhmat yatira (light and understanding and excellent wisdom) is identical to the queen's formulation in Daniel 5:11. Belshazzar has received the testimony accurately.
15: The narrative irony is made explicit: the wise men and astrologers who were brought in could not read the writing or make known the interpretation — v'peshara la-yekholeen le-hodat. The Aramaic la-yekholeen (could not) uses the same root (yakhil) that the three men used for YHWH's ability (Daniel 3:17) and that Nebuchadnezzar used in his final doxology (Daniel 4:37). The wise men lack the ability that YHWH possesses and grants to his servants.
16: v'enant shemaʿet ʿalakh di-tukhhal pishrin lemifsar (wʾănāh šĕmaʿet ʿălāk dî tûḵal pišrîn lĕmip̄sar): and I have heard of you that you can give interpretations. The conditional invitation: kean hn tukhhal khetava lemeqra (now if you can read the writing). The offer is repeated: purple, gold chain, third-ruler status. The political offer remains operative even after the wise men have failed — Belshazzar is desperate enough to offer the same package to anyone who can succeed.
17: be-edin Daniel enah umreh qodam malka (bēʾḏayin dāniyyēʾl ʿānēh wĕʾāmar qŏḏām malkāʾ): then Daniel answered and said before the king. Daniel's response opens with a formal refusal of the gifts: matnetak lakh yehavu v'yahavatakh le-akhra hab (your gifts be for yourself, give your rewards to another). This is a striking posture: Daniel refuses payment before delivering the interpretation, ensuring his independence from the court system. The interpretation will not be shaped by the reward — a principled stance that contrasts with the court wise men whose interpretations followed the money.
18: anta malka elaha ilaya malkhuta v-revuta v-iqqara v-hadar yahav le-nebukhad-netstsar avukh (ʾantāh malkāʾ ʾĕlāhāʾ ʿelyāʾ malkûṯāʾ wĕrəḇûṯāʾ wʾiqqārāʾ wĕhaḏārāʾ yĕhab lĕnĕḇûḵaḏ-neṣṣar ʾăḇûhāk): you, O king — God Most High gave Nebuchadnezzar your father kingship and greatness and glory and majesty. The fourfold listing (malkuta, revuta, iqqara, hadar = kingship, greatness, honor, glory) uses all the royal-dignity vocabulary of Daniel. The attribution to God Most High (elaha ilaya) establishes immediately that Nebuchadnezzar's power was gift, not achievement — the ground for Belshazzar's guilt in Daniel 5:22.
19: min-qdam revuteh di-yahav leh kol-amayya umevaya ulishanaya hayu zaʿin v-daylin (min-qŏḏam gəḇûrəṯēh … kol-ʿammayyāʾ ûmĕʾayyāʾ wĕliššānayyāʾ): before his greatness that he gave him, all peoples, nations, and languages trembled and feared before him. The triadic formula of peoples-nations-languages appears again (cf. Daniel 3:4; Daniel 4:1) — the scope of Nebuchadnezzar's terror was universal. V-di hava tsave haval (and whom he willed he killed): the absolute power is described without euphemism. This absolute power was YHWH's gift — which makes its abuse in Belshazzar all the more culpable (Daniel 5:22).
20: v-kivat levaveh v-ruaheh itkeshiat lihzada (wûkḇar libbēhî wĕrûḥēhî tiqĕp̄at lĕhazāḏāʾ): and when his heart was lifted up and his spirit became proud. The Aramaic kevat (hardened/stiffened) of the heart is a strong image — the heart becomes rigid, inflexible. The verb tikhep (became strong/proud) uses the same root as tikhep in the tree-image (“became strong,” Daniel 4:11) — Nebuchadnezzar's strength became Nebuchadnezzar's pride, and now it is the cause of his fall. The parallel to Belshazzar makes the typological point: the pattern of pride-judgment repeats.
21: Daniel's summary of Nebuchadnezzar's humiliation, echoing Daniel 4: min-benei anasha tetarad (driven from among men), levaveh im-chayata shevi (his heart was made like the animals), im-radana manveh (his dwelling was with wild asses). The purpose: di-yidaʿ di-shalit elaha ilaya b'malkhut anasha (that he might know that God Most High rules in the kingdom of men). The theological thesis is restated for the fifth time in Daniel 4-5: Nebuchadnezzar learned it the hard way; Belshazzar had the testimony of that lesson and ignored it.
22: v-ant bereh Belshatzar la-heshpalat levavakh (wʾantāh bĕrēhî bēlšaṣṣar lāʾ heš-pālət libbāk): and you his son Belshazzar have not humbled your heart. This is the indictment. The verb heshpalat (humbled) uses the Haphel stem of the root shapel (to be low/humble) — the same root used throughout Daniel for YHWH's ability to humble the proud (Daniel 4:37). The conjunction kol-qobel di-kol-dena yadaʿta (though you knew all this) makes the guilt inexcusable: unlike the nations who had no testimony, Belshazzar had the full record of Nebuchadnezzar's experience and disregarded it.
23: v-ʿal mar-shamayya hitromamta (wʾal māraʾ-šemayyāʾ hitrommāmta): and against the Lord of Heaven you have exalted yourself. The verb hitromamta (exalted yourself) uses the Hitpael of ram (to be high) — reflexive self-exaltation against the Lord of Heaven (mar-shamayya, a title for YHWH found only here in Daniel and Ezra 1:2). The three desecrations named: manohi di-veiteh (vessels of his house), veant verabrevanakh shegalatakh v-nilkhatakh shatin bahon (and you and your nobles and wives drinking from them), velilahei kaspa vedahava … shabbacht (and you have praised gods of silver and gold). The triple indictment escalates: desecrating what is holy, using the holy for debauchery, replacing YHWH with material gods.
24: min-kodamoh shallikh pasa yada (min-qŏḏāmôhî šĕliḥ pāsāʾ yādāʾ): from his presence was sent the hand, the palm. The Aramaic pasa (palm of the hand) is specific — not a full hand, just the palm with fingers. Shallikh (was sent) uses the divine passive: God sent it. The writing that was inscribed: kehtava dena resham (this writing was inscribed). The divine authorship of the writing is stated without qualification — the question is only whether anyone can read it.
25: The inscription: mene mene teqel ufarsin (mĕnēʾ mĕnēʾ tĕqēl ûp̄arsîn). These are Aramaic words with multiple semantic registers. Mene is from the root mana (to count/number/assign). Teqel is from teqal (to weigh). Parsin is the plural of peres (half-mina, or to divide) — also a pun on Paras (Persia). The doubling of mene may intensify the meaning (numbered, numbered — it is truly numbered), paralleling the doubled dreams of Genesis (Daniel 41:32: “the dream was doubled because the thing is fixed by God”). The inscription can also be read as a set of monetary units (mina, mina, shekel, and half-minas) — devaluing kingdoms in a monetary metaphor.
26: dena peshara milta mene (dĕnāh p̄išrāh millĕṯāʾ mĕnēʾ): this is the interpretation of the word — mene. Manah elaha malkhutakh v-hashlamah (God has numbered your kingdom and brought it to an end). The verb manah (to number/count) is completed action: it is done, the count is finished. The Hiphil hashlamah (brought to completion/an end) uses shalam — the same root as shalom (peace/wholeness/completeness). The irony: YHWH brings Belshazzar's kingdom to shalom — its complete end, its final state — using the peace-vocabulary.
27: teqel tqilta b-moznaya v-hishtkakhat khasir (tĕqēl tĕqîltāh bĕmôznayyāʾ wĕhišṭĕkaḥat ḥassîr): teqel — you have been weighed in the balances and found wanting. The passive tqilta (you were weighed) and hishtkakhat (you were found) both use divine passives. The balance/scale (moznayyah) is the judicial metaphor for divine assessment — cf. Psalms 62:9 (men are a breath, in the balances they go up). Khasir (deficient/lacking/wanting): the verdict is not that he is evil but that he is insufficient — his kingdom lacks the weight, the substance, to endure. The moral and political assessment coincide in Aramaic khasir.
28: peres peliget malkhutakh (pĕrēs pĕligat malkûṯāk): peres — your kingdom is divided. The singular peres (divide) now applies to both the monetary unit (half) and the geopolitical act. V-yehivat liMadai u-Pharas (and given to the Medes and Persians): the pun is complete — peres (divide) = Paras (Persia). The kingdom that praised the gods of gold, silver, bronze, iron, wood, and stone (Daniel 5:4) is itself divided like a coin. Daniel's interpretation converts three monetary nouns into three judicial sentences: counted, weighed, divided — a complete legal verdict in three words.
29: Belshazzar's response: he honors Daniel with the purple, chain, and proclamation of third-ruler status — the promised reward (Daniel 5:7) is paid even though the interpretation is a death sentence. This is a peculiar detail that rings historically true: the king honors the interpreter despite the interpretation. The Aramaic v-karaz ʿalohi (and they proclaimed concerning him) uses the same verb karaz (herald/proclaim) as the herald in Daniel 3:4 — the same word that announced the command to worship the image now announces the elevation of the man who refused to be ruled by the system that image represented.
30: beh be-leilya qetil Belshatzar (bêhî balēlyāʾ qĕṭîl bēlšaṣṣar): that very night Belshazzar the Chaldean king was killed. The Aramaic beh be-leilya (that very night) uses the doubled temporal expression for absolute immediacy — the same night, not the next day. Qetil (was killed) is the Peal passive — the simple statement of fact, without dramatic elaboration. The word kasday malka (Chaldean king) is the narrator's final epitaph — he is remembered as a Chaldean king, not as a god-fearer. The historical record: Xenophon's Cyropaedia and Herodotus both mention the capture of Babylon during a night feast.
31: v-Daryavesh Madaya qabbel malkhuta (wĕdāryāwēš māḏāʾāh qabbēl malkûṯāʾ): and Darius the Mede received the kingdom. The verb qabbel (received/accepted) — the kingdom passes, not by conquest described dramatically, but by quiet transfer. Bar-shinin shittin u-tarteen (being about sixty-two years old): the specific age is either a historical detail or a number significant for the narrative (sixty-two years after Nebuchadnezzar's accession would correspond roughly to 561 BCE; the narrative would then be ca. 539 BCE, within 22 years). The chapter ends not with Cyrus but with Darius the Mede — the figure around whom the next narrative (Daniel 6) will turn.
